tree_topic: Games > Computer & Video Games > Gaming Media & Reference
questions: In the history of the GameFAQs Character Battle tournaments, there is a unique instance where the ultimate champion was a tetromino rather than a traditional character. Identify the two specific opponents this inanimate champion faced and defeated in the Semi-Final and Grand Final rounds of that tournament. For both opponents, provide their full name, the video game franchise they represent, and the exact vote percentage they received in those specific losing matches.

Throughout the history of the annual 'Character Battle' tournaments hosted on GameFAQs, the main protagonist of Final Fantasy VII has been a frequent finalist. However, he was defeated in the Grand Final round on three separate occasions during the 'classic' era of the contests (2002–2006). Identify the three specific characters who defeated him in these final matches. For each of these three victors, report the specific year of their victory, the exact percentage of the vote they received in the final match (rounded to two decimal places), and the total number of votes cast for the winner in that specific matchup.

rounds.question: In the history of the GameFAQs Character Battle tournaments, there is a unique instance where the ultimate champion was a tetromino rather than a traditional character. Identify the two specific opponents this inanimate champion faced and defeated in the Semi-Final and Grand Final rounds of that tournament. For both opponents, provide their full name, the video game franchise they represent, and the exact vote percentage they received in those specific losing matches.
checklist_depth: Correctly identifies the winner as the L-Block (or L-Piece) from Tetris.
Correctly identifies the context as Character Battle VI (2007).
Correctly distinguishes between the Semi-Final opponent (Snake) and Final opponent (Cloud).
rounds.rationale: The question uses Deep Logic by describing the winner only by its nature ('tetromino'), forcing the agent to reason that this refers to the 'L-Block' and identify the specific year (2007/Character Battle VI). It then applies Wide Logic by requiring the agent to traverse the tournament bracket to locate two different entities (the opponents in the last two rounds) and aggregate specific statistical data (vote percentages) that are often found in detailed result tables rather than general summaries.
source_nodes: JP Dokkan Battle Discussion #125: Ryuken - Dragon Ball Z Dokkan Battle
rounds.answer_a:
In the 5th Annual GameFAQs Character Battle of 2006, the L-Shaped Block from the *Tetris* franchise achieved its unique victory by defeating two highly popular characters in the tournament's final stages [1].

In the Semi-Final round, the tetromino faced **Link**, the protagonist of *The Legend of Zelda* franchise. The battle was exceptionally close, with Link ultimately being defeated after garnering just **49.45%** of the vote [2]. This narrow victory propelled the inanimate block into the final match.

For the Grand Final championship, the L-Block's opponent was **Solid Snake**, the renowned special operations soldier from the *Metal Gear* franchise. In the decisive contest, Solid Snake was unable to overcome the block's momentum, losing the final round after receiving **47.39%** of the vote and securing the L-Block's historic win [1, 3].

## References

1.  GameFAQs Wiki (Fandom). (n.d.). *Character Battle V*. Retrieved September 2023, from gamefaqs.fandom.com/wiki/Character_Battle_V
2.  The Let's Play Archive. (2009, August 1). *History of the GameFAQs Character Battles*. Retrieved September 2023, from lparchive.org/History-of-the-GameFAQs-Character-Battles/Update%2006/
3.  GameFAQs. (2006). *The 5th Annual GameFAQs Character Battle*. Archived bracket data. Retrieved September 2023, from gamefaqs.gamespot.com/features/c_battle_v
rounds.answer_b:
Semi-Final: Link, the iconic hero of The Legend of Zelda series, was eliminated by the Tetris L-Block when he managed only 45.8 % of the vote, compared with the tetromino’s 54.2 % share that secured the block a place in the championship match [1].  

Grand Final: Cloud Strife, protagonist of Final Fantasy VII, became the last obstacle for the L-Block and fell short as well, drawing 44.4 % of the ballots while the inanimate challenger claimed victory with 55.6 % [2].  

These two defeats—Link’s in the semi-final and Cloud’s in the deciding round—mark the only time a non-character object captured the GameFAQs Character Battle crown, underscoring how the community’s sense of humor and surprise campaigning briefly dethroned two of gaming’s most celebrated heroes.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, we verify the Deep Logic/Ground Truth. The L-Block won **Character Battle VI (2007)**. Its path to victory was defeating **Solid Snake** in the Semi-Finals and **Cloud Strife** in the Grand Finals.

**Agent A** fails significantly on Deep Logic. It cites the wrong tournament (Battle V, 2006), claims the Semi-Final opponent was Link (incorrect), and claims the Grand Final opponent was Solid Snake (incorrect; Snake was the semi-finalist). It presents a completely erroneous history.

**Agent B** is partially correct. It correctly identifies **Cloud Strife** as the Grand Final opponent. However, it also hallucinates that the Semi-Final opponent was Link (it was Solid Snake). 

While Agent B has a formatting error (citation markers included but no reference list provided) and misses the Semi-Final entity, it is the only agent to correctly identify the Grand Finalist. Agent A provides a well-formatted but factually hallucinated answer regarding the specific opponents and tournament year. Therefore, Agent B is better for User Utility as it gets the primary final matchup correct.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: DEEP.

rounds.question: Throughout the history of the annual 'Character Battle' tournaments hosted on GameFAQs, the main protagonist of Final Fantasy VII has been a frequent finalist. However, he was defeated in the Grand Final round on three separate occasions during the 'classic' era of the contests (2002–2006). Identify the three specific characters who defeated him in these final matches. For each of these three victors, report the specific year of their victory, the exact percentage of the vote they received in the final match (rounded to two decimal places), and the total number of votes cast for the winner in that specific matchup.
checklist_width: Year of victory for Victor 3 (Leon S. Kennedy)
checklist_depth: Target Entity 3: Leon S. Kennedy (Defeated Cloud in 2005/CB4)
rounds.rationale: The question uses Deep Logic by masking the central figure (Cloud Strife) and the concept of the 'Character Battle' history, requiring the agent to identify the specific tournament brackets where Cloud lost in the finals. It uses Wide Logic by requiring the aggregation of granular statistical data (percentages and vote counts) from three distinct years (2002, 2004, 2005) which are rarely found on a single summary page.

Inazuma Eleven: Victory Road for PlayStation 5 - GameFAQs
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]:  [Wide Logic - Target 1]:  [Wide Logic - Target 2]:
rounds.answer_a:
Throughout the history of the annual GameFAQs Character Battles, Cloud Strife of *Final Fantasy VII* was defeated in the Grand Final four times during the 2002–2006 period by three unique opponents [1].

The first character to defeat Cloud in a final round was **Link**, the hero of *The Legend of Zelda* franchise. This occurred during the inaugural tournament in **2002**, where Link captured the championship with **51.99%** of the vote [2]. The total number of votes cast for Link in this specific matchup was **42,750** [2]. Link is the only character to have defeated Cloud in two separate Grand Finals, having won a rematch against him in the 2003 tournament as well [1, 2].

In the **2004** Character Battle III, Cloud was defeated by **Chrono**, the silent protagonist from the classic RPG *Chrono Trigger*. In the final round, Chrono secured the title by earning **52.00%** of the overall vote [2]. His victory was decided by a total of **44,258** votes cast in his favor, preventing Cloud from claiming the championship for the third consecutive year he reached the finals [1, 2].

Cloud’s third and final defeat in a Grand Final during this era came in **2005** at the hands of a fellow *Final Fantasy VII* character, **Vincent Valentine**. In an extremely narrow contest that pitted the two party members against each other, Vincent was declared the winner after receiving **50.18%** of the vote [2]. The total number of votes for Vincent in his winning effort was **35,013**, marking a surprising upset against the game's main protagonist [1, 2].

## References

1.  GameFAQs Wiki (Fandom). (n.d.). *Character Battle History*. Retrieved October 2023, from gamefaqs.fandom.com/wiki/Character_Battle_History
2.  The Let's Play Archive. (2009, August 1). *History of the GameFAQs Character Battles*. Retrieved October 2023, from lparchive.org/History-of-the-GameFAQs-Character-Battles/
rounds.answer_b:
2002 – Link  
• Result: Link defeated Cloud Strife to win Character Battle I.  
• Winning share: 57.12 % of the final-round vote.  
• Winner’s raw total: 112 096 votes cast for Link. [1]

2004 – Tetris L-Block  
• Result: The orange “L-Block” tetromino upset Cloud Strife in Character Battle III’s Grand Final.  
• Winning share: 55.62 %.  
• Winner’s raw total: 130 593 votes. [2]

2006 – Link  
• Result: In Character Battle V, Link again met Cloud in the championship match and prevailed.  
• Winning share: 54.32 %.  
• Winner’s raw total: 166 101 votes. [3]

These three showings—in 2002, 2004 and 2006—mark every occasion in the classic 2002-2006 era when Cloud Strife reached the title match yet fell short, twice to The Legend of Zelda’s hero and once to the notorious Tetris L-Block.

## References  
[1] GameFAQs Character Battle I – Grand Final poll archive (Dec 2002).  
[2] GameFAQs Character Battle III – Grand Final (Dec 2004) poll data.  
[3] GameFAQs Character Battle V – Grand Final (Nov 2006) archived poll results.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents failed significantly on the Deep Logic check, missing the correct trio of victors (Sephiroth, Link, Leon S. Kennedy). Both incorrectly stated Link won in 2002 (Sephiroth won). However, Agent B is the winner because it managed to identify one correct Year-Winner pair (Link defeated Cloud in 2006), whereas Agent A provided zero correct pairs, hallucinating victories for characters who never won (Chrono, Vincent). Agent B also had superior formatting (bullet points vs. text block). Agent B's inclusion of L-Block (a 2007 winner) in 2004 was an error, but less severe than Agent A's complete fabrication of winners.
